title: Eutrophication Cascade
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive simulation tracing the cascade from nutrient input through algal bloom, oxygen depletion, and dead zone formation in a lake ecosystem

learningObjectives: Trace the step-by-step cascade from nutrient input to dead zone formation
Identify nitrogen and phosphorus as the primary nutrients driving eutrophication
Explain the role of decomposer bacteria in oxygen depletion
Connect eutrophication to real-world sources such as agricultural runoff
prerequisites: Understanding of nutrient cycles (nitrogen and phosphorus)
Basic knowledge of dissolved oxygen and aquatic life requirements

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Controlled variable testing with cascading consequences
keyQuestions: What happens to dissolved oxygen when you increase nutrient input?
Which nutrient has a stronger effect -- nitrogen or phosphorus?
Why don't fish survive in a dead zone even though there is plenty of water?
How long does it take for a lake to recover after nutrient input stops?
What human activities contribute to eutrophication?
commonMisconceptions: Students often think algal blooms directly poison fish rather than understanding the oxygen depletion mechanism
Students may not realize that reducing nutrient input does not lead to immediate recovery
Students may think eutrophication only happens in oceans, not in freshwater lakes and rivers
assessmentOpportunities: Students complete a controlled-variable data table comparing nitrogen-only vs. phosphorus-only effects
Students write a causal chain explanation connecting agricultural runoff to fish kills
Students propose and justify a management solution that interrupts the eutrophication cascade
